text: The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical port for camera access, moving the camera frequently from left to right. Babcock forceps are used to identify the ureter in the right lower quadrant, while a 5mm harmonic scalpel is employed for the line of TOLT mobilization. The patient's head is oriented towards the right side of the screen, with feet towards the left, and the right ureter is reflected down.

The surgeon uses two ports on the left side for right-hand mobilization and two ports on the right side for left-hand dissection. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect around the hepatic flexure. The surgeon shows the right ureter and clears tissue from underneath it, proceeding to divide vessels for the total proctocolectomy. The ENSEAL instrument is used to take the gastrocolic omentum, followed by the stapler with an endoscopic linear cutter to address the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon is seen mobilizing the right colon and proceeding along the left side of the colon using a harmonic scalpel for precise dissection. The spleen and splenic flexure are visible towards the left upper quadrant of the screen. The left ureter is identified as the dissection progresses from the rectosigmoid junction towards the splenic flexure. The procedure is performed entirely laparoscopically, with the small bowel visible under the mesentery as vessels are divided. The surgeon uses energy sources for most of the dissection, with vascular loads applied to the inferior mesenteric, ileocolic, and middle colic vessels. The left colon is shown under the left colonic mesentery, and the middle colic artery is the last to be addressed.

The surgeon attempts hemostasis using various techniques after the stapler and clips fail, successfully achieving it with an endo-loop. The procedure progresses into the pelvis, entering the pre-sacral space. The dissection follows the avascular plane in front of the sacrum and behind the mesorectum, revealing areolar tissue posteriorly. The anus is oriented towards the top midline of the screen, with the patient's left side towards the left.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the left side, carefully avoiding the presacral space and mesorectum while working in the areolar plane. Traction and counter-traction are applied to reflect a small nerve laterally, visible on the left-hand side of the screen. The uterus, which is large and obstructive, is held up with a clamp to maintain visualization. The surgeon continues dissection posteriorly and anteriorly, maneuvering to keep the uterus out of the surgical field.

The surgeon transects the rectum and prepares to extract the specimen through the perineum. A blue load is used to create an ileostomy, ensuring the vascular pedicle on the ileocolic arcade is hemostatic and the ileum reaches the ileostomy site without tension. The intersphincteric plane is navigated, allowing the entire colon, omentum, and terminal ileum to be extracted. The right colon and omentum are visible as they emerge, completing the specimen removal.
